nativeRange.habitat: Still and slow-moving freshwater bodies including ponds, lakes, ditches, canals, and slow rivers; established almost worldwide as an introduced species in temperate to tropical waters

decorPreferences: open background space for tall stems
any substrate (will root or float freely)
moderate to bright lighting for fullest growth
regular trimming to control runaway growth

goodWith: goldfish (commonly grazed and used as a goldfish/pond plant)
avoidWith: large herbivorous or boisterous cichlids that shred stems
crayfish and freshwater crabs that cut plants apart
very strong flow that breaks the brittle stems
release into any natural waterway (highly invasive in many regions)

summary: Egeria densa, commonly sold as Anacharis or Brazilian Waterweed, is a fast-growing, cold-tolerant stem plant popular as a background plant, oxygenator, and fry shelter. It roots in substrate or floats freely and is widely reported to help suppress algae by competing aggressively for nutrients.
careNotes: Egeria densa is one of the easiest and most adaptable freshwater plants in the hobby and is the species typically sold under the trade name 'Anacharis' (also called Brazilian Elodea or Brazilian Waterweed). It is native to South America (southeastern Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay) but is now naturalised on every continent except Antarctica through the aquarium trade — in many U.S. states and other regions it is illegal to sell or possess, and it should never be released into ponds, lakes, streams or any natural waterway. Distinguishing features versus its commonly-confused look-alikes: Egeria densa carries leaves in whorls of 4–6 (most often 4) per node and the leaves are 1–3 cm long with a smooth midrib; native Elodea canadensis has whorls of 3 with much shorter (≤1.5 cm) leaves, while Hydrilla verticillata has whorls of 5 with conspicuous teeth on the leaf midrib (a reliable touch test). CO2 is not required; the plant absorbs nutrients aggressively from the water column and is widely reported to be allelopathic — meaning it releases compounds that inhibit competing organisms — with multiple sources crediting it specifically with suppressing blue-green algae. The stems are brittle and shed needle-like leaves when handled roughly, when first acclimating, or after sudden water-parameter shifts — this 'needle drop' is normal and stops once the plant settles. Plant individual stems with 2–3 cm spacing, strip the lowest 5 cm of leaves before pushing into substrate, or leave bundles floating at the surface where light access is best. Egeria densa is a particularly useful stem plant for low-tech setups, fry tanks, and goldfish or pond aquariums.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward by fragmentation: cut a healthy lateral shoot of 10 cm or longer, strip the lowest leaves, and push into substrate or let it float — new white filament roots and side shoots develop within days. The plant rarely flowers in aquariums; in the wild it produces small white three-petalled flowers held above the water surface.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source. Provides species identity, height (20-30+ cm in 2 months, up to 40-100 cm), fast growth rate, low light/CO2 demand, easy care level, planting/spacing guidance, and the explicit note that the plant absorbs large amounts of nutrients and helps prevent algae.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay), temperature tolerance (59-90 F / 15-32 C), moderate light preference, easy care, no-CO2-required, background/floating placement, allelopathic nutrient-uptake effect on algae, and invasive-species shipping restrictions to 20+ U.S. states.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Hydrocharitaceae, order Alismatales), origin (southeastern Brazil), very-fast growth, medium-to-high light demand, CO2 5-40 mg/l accelerates but is not required, broad temperature range 4-30 C (optimal 20-24 C), pH 5-9, kH 2-20, and explicit confirmation the plant 'binds nutrients and competes against algae'.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder
additional.notes: Cross-checked minimum tank size (10 gal / 40 L), temperature 15-28 C, pH 6.0-8.0, gH 2-20, distinguishing whorl-count features vs Elodea canadensis (whorls of 3) and Hydrilla (whorls of 5), allelopathic suppression of blue-green algae specifically, compatibility list (good with shrimp/tetras/corys/nerite snails; avoid goldfish/koi/crayfish/large cichlids that eat or uproot it), and survival under ice but lethal freezing. Substituted for Buce Plant which returned no usable page for this species.
